---
title: "Generalized linear models - VT26 "
subtitle: "InlÃĊmningsuppgift 1, 2.5 hp"
author: 
- David MÃỳller
- Filip StrÃ¶m
- Joel SÃ¶derman
date: last-modified
format: 
  pdf: default
  html:
    self-contained: true
language: 
  title-block-author-single: " "
crossref-fig-title: "Figur"
title-block-banner-color: Primary
title-block-published: "Publicerad"
author-title: "Gruppmedlemmar"
title-block-banner: true
editor: source
---

```{r}
#| output: false
#| echo: false
library('foreign')
library('haven') 
library('labelled')

load(url("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/johankoskinen/ST304G/main/data/GSS2022selection.RData"))

load(url("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/johankoskinen/ST304G/main/data/GSS2024selection.RData"))
```

```{r}
#| output: false
#| echo: false
table(GSSdata24$pres20, useNA='always')
table(GSSdata24$pres20==2, useNA='always')


```
# Introduction
This report analyzes data from the General Social Survey (GSS) using generalized linear models. In Part A, we study which factors are associated with voting for Donald Trump in the 2020 presidential election, using data from the 2022 and 2024 surveys. In Part B, we examine the reported number of sexual partners and discuss suitable models for this type of outcome.

Throughout the report, we compare different model specifications and link functions, interpret the estimated effects, and evaluate how well the models fit the data. We also discuss missing values and other limitations where relevant.

### Note on AI use 
We used AI in a limited way to help format the output in the report, mainly by suggesting how to use knitr::kable() and similar commands to present summary() and anova() results more clearly. 

# Task 1
::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| 
model_24 <- glm( pres20==2 ~ 
                   I(sex==2) + 
                   I(owngun==1) +
                   I(degree %in% c(4,5)), 
                 data = GSSdata24,
                 family = "binomial")


knitr::kable(summary(model_24)$coefficients, digits = 3)

```
For the 2024 model, gun ownership is positively associated with voting for Trump, while having a Bachelorâs or Graduate degree is negatively associated with the outcome. The coefficient for being female is negative, but it is not statistically significant, which means that we do not find clear evidence of a gender effect in this model.


```{r}
#| echo: false
model_22 <- glm((pres20 == 2) ~ 
                  I(sex == 2) + 
                  I(owngun == 1) + 
                  I(degree %in% c(4,5)),
               family = "binomial",
               data = GSSdata22)

knitr::kable(summary(model_22)$coefficients, digits = 3)
```
The 2022 model shows a similar pattern. Gun ownership is again positively associated with voting for Trump, while higher education is negatively associated with the outcome. In this model, being female also has a negative coefficient, and here the effect is statistically significant.


To formally test whether the association between being female and voting for Trump changed over time, we then created a pooled dataset that combines the observations from 2022 and 2024. In the code, we first selected the variables needed for the analysis, then added a new variable called year to identify which survey year each observation comes from, and finally stacked the two datasets together using rbind(). In the combined model, we included an interaction term between I(sex == 2) and I(year == 2024). This interaction term is the key part of the code, because it tests whether the effect of being female differs between 2022 and 2024, rather than simply estimating one common effect across both years.
```{r}
#| echo: false
vars_needed <- c("pres20", "sex", "owngun", "degree")

GSSdata22_sub <- GSSdata22[, vars_needed]
GSSdata24_sub <- GSSdata24[, vars_needed]

GSSdata22_sub$year <- 2022
GSSdata24_sub$year <- 2024

GSSdata <- rbind(GSSdata22_sub, GSSdata24_sub)

model <- glm(
  I(pres20 == 2) ~ .,
  data = GSSdata,
  family = binomial
)

knitr::kable(summary(model)$coefficients, digits = 3)
```
The p-value for the interaction term is 0.787. Since this p-value is large, there is no statistical evidence that the association between being female and voting for Trump changed between the two years. Therefore, we cannot reject the null hypothesis that the effect of being female is the same in 2022 and 2024. 

# Task 2:
We estimated four models, a small and a large specification, each with both a logit and a probit link. In the code, we first defined a set of variables that are used in at least one of the models. We then created GSS_clean by applying na.omit() to these variables, which means that all four models are estimated on the same complete cases. This is important, because it makes the comparison between models more fair. If the models were estimated on different subsets of the data, differences in AIC or model fit could partly reflect differences in the sample rather than differences in the models themselves.

The smaller models include only sex, gun ownership, and education, while the larger models also include region, race, type of community, and logged income. We estimated both logit and probit versions in order to compare whether the results depend on the choice of link function. We then used summary() to inspect the coefficients, anova() to compare the smaller and larger models formally, and AIC() to compare overall model fit. Finally, we used nobs() to verify that all four models were estimated on the same number of observations.

```{r}
#| output: false
#| echo: false
vars_needed <- c(
  "pres20", "sex", "owngun", "degree",
  "region", "race", "res16", "realinc"
)

# BehÃċll bara kompletta observationer
GSS_clean <- na.omit(GSSdata24[, vars_needed])

# Small logit
m_small_logit <- glm(
  pres20 == 2 ~
    I(sex == 2) +
    I(owngun == 1) +
    I(degree %in% c(4, 5)),
  data = GSS_clean,
  family = binomial(link = "logit")
)

# Big logit
m_big_logit <- glm(
  pres20 == 2 ~
    .,
  data = GSS_clean,
  family = binomial(link = "logit")
)

# Small probit
m_small_probit <- glm(
  pres20 == 2 ~
    I(sex == 2) +
    I(owngun == 1) +
    I(degree %in% c(4, 5)),
  data = GSS_clean,
  family = binomial(link = "probit")
)

# Big probit
m_big_probit <- glm(
  pres20 == 2 ~
    .,
  data = GSS_clean,
  family = binomial(link = "probit")
)

```

### Small logit model

```{r}
#| echo: false
knitr::kable(summary(m_small_logit)$coefficients, digits = 3)
```
The small logit model shows that gun ownership is positively associated with voting for Trump, while having a Bachelorâs or Graduate degree is negatively associated with the outcome. The coefficient for being female is negative, but it is not statistically significant, which means that we do not find clear evidence of a gender effect in the smaller logit specification.

### Big logit model
```{r}
#| echo: false
knitr::kable(summary(m_big_logit)$coefficients, digits = 3)

```
In the larger logit model, the main pattern remains similar. Gun ownership is still positively associated with voting for Trump, while higher education is negatively associated with the outcome. After adding region, race, type of community, and income, the model captures more variation in the data, suggesting that these background variables are also important for explaining Trump voting.


### Small probit model
```{r}
#| echo: false
knitr::kable(summary(m_small_probit)$coefficients, digits = 3)
```
The small probit model gives results that are very similar to the small logit model. Gun ownership is again positively associated with voting for Trump, while higher education is negatively associated with the outcome. The effect of being female remains negative, but not statistically significant.

### Big probit model
```{r}
#| echo: false
knitr::kable(summary(m_big_probit)$coefficients, digits = 3)
```
The large probit model also shows a pattern similar to the large logit model. The signs and significance levels of the main variables are broadly consistent across the two link functions, which suggests that our conclusions are not very sensitive to whether we use a logit or a probit link.


### ANOVA: Small vs Large Logit Model
::: {.columns}
```{r}
#| echo: false
knitr::kable(as.data.frame(anova(m_small_logit, m_big_logit, test = "Chisq")), 
             digits = 3)
```

### ANOVA: Small vs Large probit Model
```{r}
#| echo: false
knitr::kable(as.data.frame(anova(m_small_probit, m_big_probit, 
                                 test = "Chisq")), digits = 3)
```
The ANOVA results show that the larger model improves the fit significantly compared with the smaller model, both for the logit and probit specifications. This suggests that the additional variables contribute useful explanatory information and should be included in the final model.

### AIC comparison of the four models 
```{r}
#| echo: false
aic_tab <- AIC(m_small_logit, m_big_logit, m_small_probit, m_big_probit)
rownames(aic_tab) <- c("Small logit", "Large logit", "Small probit", 
                       "Large probit")

knitr::kable(aic_tab[, c("df", "AIC")], digits = 3)


```
Overall, the results suggest that demographic and social background variables are important for explaining Trump voting in 2020. In particular, gun ownership is associated with a higher probability of voting for Trump, while higher education is associated with a lower probability. Both the ANOVA comparisons and the AIC values show that the larger models fit the data better than the smaller models. Since the large logit model has the lowest AIC and is easier to interpret than the corresponding probit model, we choose it as our preferred final model. The similarity between the logit and probit results also suggests that the main conclusions are robust to the choice of link function.

```{r}
#| output: false
#| echo: false
nobs(m_small_logit)
nobs(m_big_logit)
nobs(m_small_probit)
nobs(m_big_probit)
```


# Task 3

Anteckning, tas bort sen: MIDWEST = 2

```{r}
newdata <- data.frame(
  sex = 2, 
  owngun = 1,
  degree = 4,
  region = factor(2),
  race = factor(1),
  res16 = factor(2),
  realinc = 45000)
```


# Task 3 - Joel
To answer this task, we first created a new dataset where we only kept observations with complete information on the variables used in our models: sex, gun ownership, education, region, race, type of community, and income. We did this by using complete.cases(...), which checks which rows have no missing values (NA) on these variables. This step is necessary because the models cannot make predictions for individuals if some of the explanatory variables are missing.

After this, we selected the individuals in this cleaned dataset who belong to the Midwest and who did not answer the question about the 2020 presidential election. In the dataset, Midwest is coded as region == 2, so we use this condition to identify that region. At the same time, we use is.na(pres20) to find the non-respondents, that is, the individuals with a missing value on the outcome variable. The symbol & is used to combine these conditions, meaning that both must be true at the same time: the person must both live in the Midwest and have a missing value on pres20.

Finally, we used our two models, the larger logit model and the larger probit model, to predict the probability that each individual in this group voted for Trump. We then took the average of these predicted probabilities. This gives us a model-based estimate of the proportion of Trump voters among the non-respondents in the Midwest.


```{r}
#| echo: false
GSS_pred <- GSSdata24[
  complete.cases(GSSdata24[, c("sex", "owngun", "degree", "region", 
                               "race", "res16", "realinc")]),
]

midwest_nr <- GSS_pred[GSS_pred$region == 2 & is.na(GSS_pred$pres20), ]

logit_est <- mean(predict(m_big_logit, newdata = midwest_nr, 
                          type = "response"))
probit_est <- mean(predict(m_big_probit, newdata = midwest_nr, 
                           type = "response"))

pred_tab <- data.frame(
  Model = c("Large logit", "Large probit"),
  Estimated_rpercent = c(logit_est, probit_est) * 100
)

knitr::kable(pred_tab, digits = 1)


```
The large logit model estimates that approximately 31.6 percent of the non-respondents in the Midwest voted for Trump, while the large probit model gives a very similar estimate of 31.8 percent. Since the two estimates are almost identical, the result appears robust to the choice of link function. We therefore conclude that the predicted proportion of Trump voters among non-respondents in the Midwest is about 32 percent.



Part B Kommentarer
- Saknar odds ratio
- Deviation korrekt?
- Mer latex som stÃĊmmer Ã¶verrens med frl anteckningarna. 
- Assesment of fit
- Skriv om Zelterman
- Se kommentarer i text. 
- Skriv om till engelska.
- AnvÃĊnda probit link?
- AIC vÃĊrden
- Figurer nÃĊr vi predicierar
- Skriva att poission inte ÃĊr bÃĊttre ÃĊn logit (mean = var)
- Goodness of fit test

# Part B
### Task 4
```{r}
table(GSSdata24$partners , useNA = 'always')
```
Variabeln partners beskriver antal partners som den tillfrÃċgade har rapporterat. Variabeln ÃĊr delvis censorerad dÃċ vissa kategorier representerar ett intervall (ex 5 som motsvarar 5-10 i antal) snarare ÃĊn ett exakt antal. FÃ¶r att hantera detta problem behÃ¶vde vi approximera ett ungefÃĊrligt exakt vÃĊrde fÃ¶r dessa intervall. Vi ansatte kategori 5 till 7 stycken (i antal) dÃċ detta lÃċg nÃĊra ett "medel" i kategorin samt 12 fÃ¶r kategorin 11+ (ligger nÃĊra 11). Kategori 9 innebÃĊr "ospecificierat" och vi valde att sÃĊtta denna till NA (saknat vÃĊrden fÃ¶r att inte snedvrida resultatet). 

Sedermera valde vi att ta bort de saknade vÃĊrderna frÃċn vÃċrt dataset dÃċ GLM modeller krÃĊver observerade responsvÃĊrden. Dessutom sÃċ baseras GLM estimeringar pÃċ likelihoodfunktioner vilka krÃĊver observerade utfall. GLM-modeller kan heller inte modellera censorerad data vilket sÃċledes gÃ¶r att vi mÃċste approximera intervallen fÃ¶r att mÃ¶jliggÃ¶ra likelihood estimationer. 

MÃĊtfel:
I och med att vi approximerar de grupperade variablerna sÃċ tillkommer det mÃĊtfel, dessa kan vi inte udgÃċs men fÃ¶r att mÃ¶jliggÃ¶ra GLM krÃĊvs dessa antaganden. Detta dÃċ vi dels vidhÃċller samma ordning i datan samt att vi fÃ¶rhÃċller oss till "top coded" problematiken. HÃĊmtat frÃċn Wikipedia (2020-12-02, "Top-coded"): "To help overcome this problem, CPS provides the mean value of top coded values". 

Vi skapar ÃĊven en binÃĊr variabel "married" fÃ¶r att kunna undersÃ¶ka om ÃĊktenskap pÃċverkar antal partners: TRUE = gift, FALSE = ej gift. 

```{r}
dat <- GSSdata24
dat$partners_num <- dat$partners

dat$partners_num[dat$partners == 5] <- 7
dat$partners_num[dat$partners == 6] <- 12
dat$partners_num[dat$partners == 9] <- NA

# Kanske lite lÃĊngre fÃ¶rklaring till denna kod-del. 
dat <- subset(dat, !is.na(partners_num))

dat$married <- dat$marital == 1
```

### LÃĊnkfunktion 1 - Poission Log-link:

Eftersom att responsvariabeln representerar antal har vi valt att anvÃĊnda en poisson regression med log lÃĊnk. 

$$log(\mu) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 * marriedTRUE$$
dÃĊr:
$$E(Y) = \mu $$
AlltsÃċ, vi modellerar fÃ¶rvÃĊntat antal partners. 
```{r}
pois_1 <- glm(partners_num ~ married,
              family = poisson(link = "log"),
              data = dat
)

summary(pois_1)

b <- coef(pois_1)

# FÃ¶rvÃĊntat antal partners fÃ¶r en ogift person
mu_unmarried <- exp(b[1]) # = 1.124277

# FÃ¶rvÃĊntat antal partners vid gift
mu_married <- exp(b[1] + b[2]) # = 1.037569

list("partners vid ogift" = mu_unmarried, "partners vid gift" = mu_married)
```

Tolkning av koefficienter:

Intercept (b[1] = 0.11714): Detta ÃĊr det logaritmerade "mu" fÃ¶r en ogift individ NÃĊr vi transformerar detta (exp) fÃċr vi ut att det fÃ¶rvÃĊntade antalet partners fÃ¶r en ogift individ uppgick till 1.124277 stycken. 

marriedTRUE (-0.08026): Detta anger skillnaden mellan att vara ogift kontra gift. Vid transformation av detta vÃĊrdet tillsammans med interceptet fÃċr vi ut ett fÃ¶rvÃĊntat vÃĊrde pÃċ 1.0376 SÃĊtt ur en procentuell vinkel sÃċ kan vi sÃĊga att gifta personer i genomsnitt har $8 \%$ fÃĊrre fÃ¶rvÃĊntade partners ÃĊn ogifta. (Se nedan varfÃ¶r vi kan gÃ¶ra denna tolkning)

p-vÃĊrde (0.0426): marriedTRUE ÃĊr signifikant pÃċ 95% nivÃċn, sÃċledes kan vi med 95% sÃĊkerhet konstatera att gifta individer har fÃĊrre partners.

Modellantagande Poisson:

$$ Var(Y) = E(Y)$$
```{r}
mean(dat$partners_num)
var(dat$partners_num)
```
Variansen (2.7833) Ã¶verstiger medelvÃĊrdet (1.0899) vilket indikerar att poissonantagandet om lika varians och medelvÃĊrde ÃĊr begrÃĊnsat. 

### LÃĊnkfunktion 2 - Logit-modeller:

$$Odds = e^{\beta}$$
$$Sannolikhet = \frac{Odds}{1 + Odds}$$
Vi skapar 2 stycken binÃĊra variabler:

- Minst en partner (any_partner)

- Fler ÃĊn en partner (many_partners)
```{r}
dat$any_partner <- dat$partners_num > 0

dat$many_partners <- dat$partners_num > 1
```

```{r}
# Logit modell - minst en partner:
logit_any <- glm(any_partner ~ married, 
                 family = binomial(link = "logit"), 
                 data = dat
                 )
summary(logit_any)

b_2 <- coef(logit_any)

odds_any_unmarried <- exp(b_2[1]) # 1.46
odds_any_married <- exp(b_2[1] + b_2[2]) # 7.238

prob_any_unmarried <- odds_any_unmarried / (1 + odds_any_unmarried) # 0.5934
prob_any_married <- odds_any_married / (1 + odds_any_married) # 0.8786

list("Prob if unmarried" = prob_any_unmarried, 
     "Prob if married" = prob_any_married)
```
Tolkning av koefficienter:

Intercept (0.378): Ãr vÃċrt logodds fÃ¶r ogifta individer som har haft minst en partner. Vid transformation av detta fÃċr vi ut oddset som uppgick till 1.46. Sedermera omvandlar vi detta till en sannolikhet som sÃĊger oss att ogifta personer har ca $59 \%$ sannolikhet att ha haft minst en partner

marriedTRUE: Ãr fÃ¶rÃĊndringen i logodds mellan individer som ÃĊr ogifta och gifta. Vi transformerar detta till odds genom att addera $\beta_1$ (1.6) med interceptet fÃ¶r att sedan exponentiera det (Odds = 7.238). Sedan omvandlar vi oddset till en sannolikhet (0.8786). HÃĊr kan det konstateras att gifta individer har ca $88\%$ sannolikhet att ha haft minst en partner, vilket sÃċledes ÃĊr mycket hÃ¶gre ÃĊn de ogifta individerna. Notering: I och med att koeficienten ÃĊr positiv vet vi att "vara gift" Ã¶kar sannolikheten att minst haft en partner, vÃĊrt att nÃĊmna ÃĊr att detta resultat ÃĊr ytterst signifikant (p-vÃĊrde: 2e-16). 

```{r}
# Logit modell - fler ÃĊn en partner:
logit_many <- glm(many_partners ~ married, 
                 family = binomial(link = "logit"), 
                 data = dat
                 )
summary(logit_many)

b_3 <- coef(logit_many)

odds_many_unmarried <- exp(b_3[1]) # 0.19076
odds_many_married <- exp(b_3[1] + b_3[2]) # 0.039

prob_many_unmarried <- odds_many_unmarried / (1 + odds_many_unmarried) # 0.16
prob_many_married <- odds_many_married / (1 + odds_many_married) # 0.0376

list("Prob if unmarried" = prob_many_unmarried, 
     "Prob if married" = prob_many_married)
```
Tolkning av koefficienter:

Intercept (-1.65673): Ãr vÃċrt logodds fÃ¶r ogifta individer som har haft fler ÃĊn en partner. Vid transformation av detta fÃċr vi ut oddset som uppgick till 0.19076. Sedermera omvandlar vi detta till en sannolikhet som sÃĊger oss att ogifta personer har ca $16 \%$ sannolikhet att ha haft fler ÃĊn en partner.

marriedTRUE: Ãr fÃ¶rÃĊndringen i logodds mellan individer som ÃĊr ogifta och gifta. Vi transformerar detta till odds (0.039) och sedan omvandlar detta till en sannolikhet (0.0376). HÃĊr kan det konstateras att gifta individer har ca $3.76\%$ sannolikhet att ha haft fler ÃĊn en partner, vilket sÃċledes ÃĊr mycket lÃĊgre ÃĊn de ogifta individerna. Notering: I och med att koeficienten ÃĊr negativ vet vi att "vara gift" minskar sannolikheten att ha haft fler ÃĊn en partner, vÃĊrt att nÃĊmna ÃĊr att detta resultat ÃĊr ytterst signifikant (p-vÃĊrde: 2e-16). 

### Prediktion (Hypotetisk respondent):
Vi betraktar en hypotetisk manlig respondent som antingen ÃĊr gift eller ogift, 35 Ãċr och undersÃ¶ker hur modellerna predicierar antal partners (poisson) respektive sannolikheten (logit). Detta gÃ¶r vi genom att skapa hypotetiska individer (new_person) som vi sedan applicerar pÃċ vÃċra modeller i en dataframe. Detta skapar en tabell som ger oss de Ã¶nskade vÃĊrderna. Eftersom att modellerna inte bygger pÃċ Ãċlder eller kÃ¶n sÃċ kommer prediktionen enbart innefatta bakgrundsvariabeln "married". 

Kommandot type = "response" transformerar modellens linjÃĊra prediktor tillbaka till dess originalskala. FÃ¶r poissonmodellen innebÃĊr det "fÃ¶rvÃĊntat antal partners", medan fÃ¶r logitmodellen innebÃĊr det sannolikhet. 

```{r}
new_person <- data.frame(married = c(FALSE, TRUE), 
                         age = 35, 
                         sex = 1)

data.frame(married = new_person$married, 
# Poisson (antal)
        antal_partners = predict(pois_1, 
        newdata = new_person, 
        type = "response"),
# Logit (Sannolikhet) > 0
        prob_any_partner = predict(logit_any,
        newdata = new_person,
        type = "response"), 
# Logit (sannolikhet) > 1
        prob_many_partners = predict(logit_many,
        newdata = new_person,
        type = "response")
)

```
Enligt poisson modellen fÃ¶rvÃĊntas en ogift individ (1.1243) rapportera nÃċgot fler partners kontra en gift individ (1.0376). Logit modellen predicerar att gifta individer (0.8786) har hÃ¶gre sannolikhet att ha haft minst en partner jÃĊmfÃ¶rt med ogifta individer (0.5934). DÃĊrtill kan det konstateras att gifta individer (0.0376) har lÃĊgre sannolikhet att ha haft fler ÃĊn en partner kontra en ogift individ (0.16). Data framen illustrerar tre olika aspekter utifrÃċn den binÃĊra variablen married.

###AIC, assesment of fit mellan modellerna:

FÃ¶r att bedÃ¶ma modellernas anpassning (model of fit) anvÃĊnder vi Akike Information Criterion (ÃĊven kallat AIC). AIC bygger pÃċ modellens maximala likelihood och straffar sedan fÃ¶r antalet fÃ¶rklaringsvariabler. 

$$ AIC = 2p-2 log (L(\widehat{\beta}))$$
```{r}
aic_tab_2 <- AIC(pois_1, logit_any, logit_many)

knitr::kable(aic_tab_2)
```
Tolkning AIC:
FÃ¶r det fÃ¶rsta ÃĊr det viktigt att belysa att modellerna inte avhandlar samma responsvariabel. Poissonmodellen modellerar antal partners och logit modellerna modellererar binÃĊra hÃĊndelser sÃċsom sannolikheten att ha haft en respektive fler ÃĊn en partner. FÃ¶r det andra bÃ¶r sÃċledes AIC i detta fall inte tolkas som en direkt jÃĊmfÃ¶relse mellan modellerna utan snarare som ett mÃċtt pÃċ hur vÃĊl de enskilda modellerna beskriver den enskilda responsen. 

De lÃċga AIC vÃĊrderna fÃ¶r logit modellerna pÃċvisar att variationen i partnerskap bÃĊttre fÃ¶rstÃċs som diskreta steg snarare ÃĊn som en enskild poissonprocess. Resultatet kan sÃċledes vara till grund fÃ¶r en tolkning dÃĊr partnerskap modellereras i tvÃċ steg:

i) Sannolikheten att ha haft minst en partner, och

ii) givet minst en partner, vad ÃĊr sannolikheten att ha fler ÃĊn en partner. 

Sammantaget visar AIC resultaten att ÃĊktenskap frÃĊmst avser sannolikheten att ha en partner och minskar sannolikheten att ha fler en ÃĊn partner, snarare ÃĊn att avse hur mÃċnga partners man har. 

### Barplot Ã¶ver antal partners:
```{r}
#| fig-width: 5
#| fig-height: 3
#| out-width: "50%"
#| fig-align: center
#| echo: false

barplot(table(dat$partners_num), 
     main = "FÃ¶rdelning Ã¶ver # partners_num", 
     xlab = "Antal partners", 
     ylab = "frekvens", 
     col = "lightblue"
     )
```

FÃ¶rdelningen av antal partners ÃĊr hÃ¶gerskev, dÃĊr mÃċnga tillfrÃċgade rapporterat lÃċga vÃĊrden och fÃċ hÃ¶ga vÃĊrden. Detta innebÃĊr att att sambandet mellan ÃĊktenskap och antalet partners inte ÃĊr homogent Ã¶ver hela fÃ¶rdelningen. Denna analys blir dÃĊremot lite tvetydig dÃċ viss del av datan ÃĊr censurerad. Vi antar dÃĊremot en hÃ¶gerskev fÃ¶rdelning. 

Med detta i Ãċtanke samt AIC utvÃĊrderingen sÃċ vidhÃċller vi den mer effektiva uppdelningen i) och ii). 

Skillnaden mellan att ha minst en partner och fler ÃĊn en visar sÃċledes att ÃĊktenskap frÃĊmst ÃĊr relaterad till sannolikheten att Ã¶verhuvudtaget ha en partner, snarare ÃĊn till ett hÃ¶gre antal partners bland individer som redan haft en partner. I och med detta kan en uppdelad modellstruktur motiveras ytterliggare. 

### Zelterman:

Detta kan kopplas till Zeltermans modell, som fokuserar pÃċ:
